FarmerIssueDescription: i see rottings on my corn

weather_context.conditions: Cloudy, cool, very humid, no recent precipitation, moderate westerly wind.
weather_context.weather_impact_analysis: Relative humidity is very high (92%) with full cloud cover, which limits drying and maintains leaf and ear surface moisture that favors sporulation and survival of ear-rot fungi on already-colonized ears. Temperatures are cool (4.7Â°C), which slows many fungi; however, Penicillium species tolerate and persist under cool, moist conditions, so existing infections can continue to develop slowly. No recent rainfall reduces splash dispersal, but moderate wind (17.3 km/h) can move dry spores once husks are disturbed. Overall, conditions sustain, but do not rapidly accelerate, blueâgreen ear rot development.

image_description.plant_condition: Pronounced blueâgreen, powdery to fuzzy mold mass at the ear tip with several tip kernels discolored, softened, and shriveled; lower kernels appear mostly sound.
image_description.environment: Field setting under cloudy skies; likely limited air movement in the canopy and slow drying.

discoloration.description: Dense blueâgreen mold growth covering the ear tip and adjacent kernels; affected kernels appear waterâsoaked to darkened and shriveled.

surface_changes.description: Powdery to fuzzy sporulation concentrated at the tip; superficial mold mats over kernels.

necrosis.extent: Moderate to severe decay localized to the upper 10â20% of the ear.

key_diagnostic_features: Vivid blueâgreen, powdery/fuzzy mold concentrated on the ear tip
Tip kernels discolored, softened, and shriveled with localized decay
Cool, very humid conditions that sustain Penicillium growth and sporulation on colonized ears

differential_diagnoses.disease_name: Aspergillus ear rot (Aspergillus flavus and others)
differential_diagnoses.likelihood: Lower: Typically oliveâyellowâgreen mold favored by hot, dry conditions; current cool, very humid weather and distinct blueâgreen coloration point away from Aspergillus.
Low: Often presents as white to pink/salmon mold with possible starburst patterns under the pericarp rather than vivid blueâgreen surface growth.
Low: Characterized by white mold that can overgrow husks with black pycnidia; not blueâgreen.

diagnosis.weather_correlation: Very high humidity and full cloud cover support persistence and sporulation of Penicillium on previously infected tips, while the cool temperature slows but does not stop progress. Absence of recent rainfall limits splash dispersal, but moderate winds can move spores if ears are disturbed, aligning with localized tip infections visible in the photo.

immediate_actions: Avoid handling or harvesting while ears are wet or humidity is extremely high to reduce spore spread and worker exposure.
Flag and, where practical, remove and destroy heavily affected ears at the field edge to limit inoculum entering the combine.
Wear an N95 or better respirator and eye protection when working around moldy grain to protect from inhalation of spores and potential mycotoxins.

treatment_options.description: Harvest fields with visible ear rot as soon as grain moisture is safe to minimize further fungal growth and mycotoxin accumulation; adjust combine air to blow out lightweight, moldy kernels and fines.
treatment_options.effectiveness: High if executed promptly; reduces further decay and contamination.
treatment_options.weather_timing: Target a drier window (lower RH, breezy conditions) later in the day after dew dries; avoid harvesting during/just after fog or drizzle.

treatment_options.description: Dry grain quickly to â¤15% moisture (preferably 13â14% for storage) and cool with aeration to inhibit Penicillium growth; promptly segregate moldy lots.
treatment_options.effectiveness: Very effective at halting further fungal development postharvest.
treatment_options.weather_timing: If ambient air remains cool and humid, use heated or controlled drying and active aeration rather than relying on natural-air drying.

treatment_options.description: Clean grain to remove fines and visibly moldy kernels; store suspect grain separately and move it first.
treatment_options.effectiveness: Moderate to high; reduces inoculum and mycotoxin concentration in marketable grain.
treatment_options.weather_timing: Do during a dry window to reduce condensation and re-wetting during handling.

treatment_options.description: Curative fungicide applications are not effective once ear rot is established; in-season ear rot management with fungicides is limited and timing-specific (e.g., silking) for other ear rots, not Penicillium.
treatment_options.effectiveness: Low for established Penicillium ear rot.

preventive_measures: Select hybrids with good ear rot tolerance and tight husk coverage; consider traits and management that limit ear-feeding insect injury.
Manage insects and birds that damage ear tips, as wounds predispose ears to Penicillium colonization.
Rotate crops and manage residue to reduce inoculum carryover.
Aim for uniform pollination and avoid stress that leads to poor tip fill; damaged or unfilled tips are more prone to colonization.
Plan harvest capacity to remove high-risk fields first and dry grain rapidly to safe moisture each season.
Optimize field airflow (e.g., avoid excessive plant density) and drainage to reduce prolonged ear wetness late in the season.
recommendations.monitoring_advice: Scout multiple areas (at least 10â20 ears per location) after damp, cool, cloudy periods from late milk through pre-harvest. Track the percentage of ears with visible tip mold. If more than 10% of ears show mold, prioritize early harvest and postharvest drying/cleaning. Test suspect lots for mycotoxins before feeding or marketing.
weather_based_recommendations: Given current very high humidity and cloud cover, wait for a drier, breezier period later in the day or the next dry window to harvest, minimizing spore spread and equipment contamination.
If the next several days remain cool and humid, plan for artificial drying and active aeration immediately after harvest to rapidly reach â¤15% (preferably 13â14%) grain moisture.
Avoid opening and handling bins on humid days to prevent condensation; move and aerate grain during cooler, drier conditions.
Increase scouting frequency following any foggy mornings or extended high-RH periods, as these prolong surface moisture on ear tips.

sources.title: Corn Ear Rots: A Guide for Identification and Management
sources.author: Crop Protection Network

sources.title: Aspergillus ear rot and aflatoxin contamination of corn
sources.author: Iowa State University Extension and Outreach

sources.title: Grain Molds and Mycotoxins in Corn
sources.author: R. L. (Bob) Nielsen, Purdue University

response.limitations: Diagnosis is based on visible blueâgreen sporulation at the ear tip and current cool, humid weather; Penicillium is most likely, but some Aspergillus species can appear green. Field images cannot determine mycotoxin levels; laboratory identification and mycotoxin testing (especially for ochratoxin/citrinin and, if applicable, aflatoxin) are recommended before feeding or marketing. The single-ear image may not represent wholeâfield severity.
